title: Shepherd the Flock
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 7-14-24 -AM- Shepherd the Flock - 1 Peter 5_1-4 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: God's Order in the Church
subpoints: God's universe is orderly — a reflection of his nature
God instituted orderly family, civil government, and church government
Elders were appointed by God to rule his church according to his Word
Summary of last week: elders are a gift from God for equipping the saints

outline.title: The Commander: Peter as a Fellow Elder (v. 1)
subpoints: Peter identifies himself as a fellow elder, not as an apostle
The office of apostle contained and included the perpetual office of elder
Peter was the ideal person to give this command — he received it himself from Christ (John 21:15-17)
Peter denied Christ three times, yet Christ restored him by asking three times: 'Do you love me? Feed my sheep'
Jesus used two different words for love — the third time lowering his standard to Peter's level — but the command remained the same

outline.title: The Command: Shepherd the Flock of God (v. 2a)
subpoints: Peter could have used apostolic authority but entreated them as a fellow elder
By this point, government had shifted from apostles to elders (Acts 15)
Calvin: 'By this name elders, Peter designates pastors and all those appointed for the government of the church'
What shepherding involves: tending, feeding, disciplining, protecting
The flock belongs to God — like the parable of the talents, elders are managers who must return it better than they found it
The chief shepherd is Christ (Psalm 23); elders follow his pattern

outline.title: How to Execute the Command: Four Contrasts (vv. 2b-3)
subpoints: Not by compulsion but willingly — the elder is not forced, though he can be encouraged
Calvin's example: William Farel persuading Calvin to stay in Geneva
Not for dishonest gain but eagerly — motivation is not financial; Paul's right: those who preach the gospel should live from it (1 Cor. 9:14), but money should never be the motive
Not as lords over those entrusted but as examples to the flock
Ezekiel 34: warning about shepherds who made the sheep serve them instead of serving the sheep
The elder's high standard is the ceiling for the whole church — every Christian should aspire to it
Also implicit: the sheep must be willing to be shepherded (Heb. 13:17)

outline.title: The Reason for the Command (1 Peter 4:17)
subpoints: 'Therefore' in v. 1 points back to 1 Peter 4:17 — judgment begins at the house of God
The church will not be much different from its leaders
As in Ezekiel 34, the entire flock was judged when the shepherds failed

outline.title: The Blessing: The Crown of Glory (v. 4)
subpoints: When the chief shepherd appears, faithful elders will receive the crown of glory
The desire of the elders of this church: to be recognized by Christ as faithful shepherds
Application: follow the chief shepherd by following those he has placed over you

summary: Preaching from 1 Peter 5:1-4, Pastor Tito Lyro continues the 1 Peter series by examining Peter's command to the elders: shepherd the flock of God. He frames the passage within his conviction that God's orderly creation is reflected in his orderly institutions — family, civil government, and the visible church — and that elders are God's provision for that order in the church. He draws attention to Peter's deliberate choice to address the elders as a fellow elder rather than as an apostle, and traces Peter's unique authority to speak on this command to his own restoration by Christ in John 21, where the three-fold 'Do you love me? Feed my sheep' commission directly parallels the three-fold denial. The heart of the sermon unpacks four contrasts in verses 2-3 that specify how the shepherd's command is to be executed — not by compulsion but willingly, not for dishonest gain but eagerly, not as lords but as examples — and closes with the reason for the command (the coming judgment of God's house, 1 Peter 4:17) and its blessed reward (the unfading crown of glory at the appearing of the chief shepherd).
transcript:
## Scripture Reading

For example, you stay standing for a minute longer as we read God's Word. We're reading from 1 Peter chapter 5, starting verse 1, through verse 11. This is the Word of our Lord:

The elders who are among you I exhort, I who am a fellow elder and a witness of the suffering of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that will be revealed. Shepherd the flock of God which is among you, serving as overseers, not by compulsion but willingly, not for dishonest gain but eagerly, nor as being lords over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock. And when the chief shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that does not fade away. Likewise you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders. Yes, all of you be submissive to one another, and be clothed with humility, for God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. Therefore humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time, casting all your care upon him for he cares for you. Be sober, be vigilant, because your adversary the devil walks about like a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour. Resist him, steadfast in the faith, knowing that the same sufferings are experienced by your brotherhood in the world. But may the God of all grace, who called us to his eternal glory by Christ Jesus, after you have suffered a while, perfect, establish, strengthen, and settle you. To him be the glory and the dominion forever and ever. Amen.

Let us pray. Father, thank you for your word. We pray that you'd help me to proclaim it in the way that's helpful to your people and brings glory to you, for asking in Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: God's Order in the Church

I hope the absence of a coat doesn't keep you from hearing the gospel this morning. We know that it was God who fashioned the universe. And that the universe is orderly as a reflection of God's nature. We can look at everything that we see — order — which makes sense, because a God of order created the universe. The Apostle Paul says in 1 Corinthians 14: for God is not the author of confusion but of peace, as in all the churches of the saints. And he says that all things be done decently and in order.

And this order is true not only of the physical universe but also the spiritual and social realms. There's order everywhere. God instituted an orderly family, where roles are clearly defined between husbands, wives, parents, and children. God instituted the civil government as a minister of order. And God instituted the visible church to be orderly governed by elders with Christ as its head. And that's the summary of the 40-minute sermon from last week — which now you might say, why did you spend 40 minutes saying it when you could have said it in two minutes?

So elders were appointed by God to rule his church according to his word in an orderly fashion. Elders were given to the church as a gift from God to help in the equipping of the saints for the work of the ministry. And as we look at this passage today — verses one through four — I want to draw your attention to the commander, the command itself, the reason for the command, and the blessing that comes from the command.

## The Commander: Peter as a Fellow Elder (v. 1)

Let's look at the commander itself. If you'll see there in chapter five verse one, Peter identifies himself as a fellow elder, writing to the elders of the churches in Cappadocia, Bithynia, Galatia, and the other regions. Now, when Peter was consecrated to the office of an apostle, he was also consecrated to the office of elder, because the office of the apostle contained the office of elder. The elements of teaching, ruling, and praying that were part of the office of the apostle are perpetuated and continued in the office of the elder.

And I think it's very interesting that Peter says that he's writing to them as a fellow elder. He could have said he's writing as an apostle — as an apostle, they were bound to obey him. And yet he claimed what he considered a higher perpetual office: the office of the elder, as a reason why the church should listen to him.

And Peter was the ideal person to give this command to shepherd the flock of Christ. He received this command himself from Christ. As you remember, Christ had prophesied that before the rooster crowed, Peter would have denied him three times. And that happened. And though Peter was excited by the resurrection of Christ, as you read the gospels, you still see there's some distance there between Peter and Christ, till you get to John 21, where Jesus brings Peter out from among the other apostles and restores him back to usefulness to the gospel of Jesus Christ.

That conversation goes like this in John 21:15-17: Jesus asks Peter, Simon, son of Jonah, do you love me more than these? He said to him, yes, Lord, you know that I love you. Then Jesus responds, feed my lambs. Jesus again asks, Simon, son of Jonah, do you love me? Peter replies, yes, Lord, you know that I love you. Jesus tells him, tend my sheep. And then Jesus for the third time asks, Simon, son of Jonah, do you love me? Peter was grieved because he said to him the third time, do you love me? And he said to him, Lord, you know all things. You know that I love you. Jesus said to him, feed my sheep.

Now something that stands out as we read John 21 is that Jesus uses one word for love and Peter uses a different word for love. Jesus asks, Peter, do you love me supremely? And Peter answers, Lord, I love you like a brother. Second time, Peter, do you love me supremely? Peter responds, Lord, I love you like a brother. And then the third time, Jesus says, Peter, do you love me like a brother? And Peter is grieved now because Jesus knows his heart, he knows all things. And he knows that Peter doesn't love him supremely. But Jesus is willing to work with that. Peter, do you love me like a brother? Yes, Lord, you know that's the case. Well, the instructions are the same. Shepherd my sheep.

So what Peter is asking the elders to do is what Christ commissioned him as an elder to do — to shepherd the sheep.

## The Command: Shepherd the Flock of God (v. 2)

And we come then to look at the command itself: shepherd the flock. Notice that Peter could have used apostolic authority, but he didn't. He entreated them like a fellow elder who has gone through the same experiences, who knows what it is to be an elder. And by this time in the history of the New Testament, there had been a shift already from government by apostles to government by elders in the church. We see that shift in Acts chapter 15. And this is a command given to elders in general. John Calvin says, by this name elders, Peter designates pastors and all those who are appointed for the government of the church. So Peter speaks to all classes of elders without distinction, both ruling and teaching elders.

Now, as we saw last week, the one office of the elder has different functions — namely the function of ruling, and the function of teaching. Paul clearly makes that distinction when he speaks to Timothy: all elders who rule are worthy of honor, and especially those who have been satisfied by the other elders, by the church, and by Christ himself, to labor in word and doctrine — 1 Timothy 5:17-18.

So Peter tells the elders: shepherd the flock. The word shepherd here is a command, an order, not a suggestion. And this command is not peculiar to Peter. Paul, in his sermon to the Ephesian elders in Acts 20:28-29, says this: take heed to yourselves and to all the flock among which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers to shepherd the church of God, which he purchased with his own blood. For I know this, that after my departure savage wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock. So shepherd the flock is the duty of the elders. That's why elders are here.

And notice whose flock it is. Peter says shepherd the flock of God. The sheep don't belong to the elders but to God. This is much like the parable of the talents — someone's given five talents to manage, given two talents, given one talent. They are told to manage and to return them to the Lord. And that's what the flock of God is. The flock of God is entrusted to the elders to manage and then to return to the Lord better than what they found it.

So elders must deal with the people in the church as the sheep of God who will have to be presented to God at his coming. We also need to consider what it means to shepherd. There are several elements: tending, caring for or looking after, giving one's attention to; feeding, giving them what's needed for growth in this context, which means to equip them to serve the Lord, to do the work of the ministry; and disciplining — protecting them, keeping them in the place where they are to go. And the best way to figure out how to shepherd is by looking at the chief shepherd, the Lord Jesus Christ. We see that in Psalm 23:1-3, which we read as a responsive reading: the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want. He makes me to lie down in green pastures. He leads me besides the still waters. He restores my soul. He leads me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake.

Often when we're praying here before the service, we pray that the Lord uses the elders of the church to lead the congregation beside still waters and to green pastures in the Word of God. The Lord Jesus Christ says that he is the shepherd of the sheep, and one of the ways that he shepherds the sheep is by laying his life down for them. And that's how we as elders are to shepherd our sheep as well — they are to be of such importance to us that we're willing to lay down our lives for them.

## How to Execute the Command: Four Contrasts (vv. 2-3)

Besides the general idea of shepherding, our passage also gives us specific directions on executing the command in particular for the elders. The first thing Peter says is that shepherding is done by overseeing. The word here — serving as an overseer — is the same word from which we get the word bishop. Nick, I'm informed that Nick, who is filling in for me next week, is going to start wearing a pointy hat for church. So I'll call him Bishop Anderson. Because an elder is a bishop — these are synonymous things. Serving as an overseer means exactly what it says: to be over, to manage, to care for a group. And notice that the object of the overseer is the flock among you. The elders should be involved in broader church work, but their primary focus is the local church. I use this to manage my own time: if one of you from this church calls me, I have time for you, whatever else is happening. I will drop anything. I am never too busy for you. And I hope that you never keep from contacting one of the elders because you think we're too busy — because we're never too busy to shepherd the flock.

Peter then continues by using several contrasts to specify how this overseen work is to be done.

First: not by compulsion but willingly. An elder is not to be forced into the office. There can be persuasion, but not compulsion. The most classic example of persuasion guiding someone into the office of elder is John Calvin. After he was chased out of France, his desire was just to be a guy in the back room reading books and writing books. Because of the war, he couldn't take the straight route to Strasbourg — he had to go around and stop in Geneva, Switzerland. In Geneva, a man named William Farel finds out Calvin's in town. Farel says, Calvin, God loves you and I have a wonderful plan for your life. You're going to stay here in Geneva and you're going to pastor the church in Geneva. Calvin says, no, no, I'm going to go to Strasbourg and hang out with my buddy Martin Bucer and write things together. And Farel says, I'll call a curse of God upon you if you leave. Calvin says, okay, I'll stay. And the rest is history.

Second: not for dishonest gain but eagerly. A reference to some of the elders being paid by the church. Peter says, don't shepherd the flock of God because you want to benefit from it. Now, this is not a prohibition from paying some of the elders, as we saw in 1 Timothy 5:17-18 and 1 Corinthians 9:14, where Paul says the Lord has commanded that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel. So it's okay to pay elders, it's okay to pay pastors. But the motivation should never be financial gain. Nobody should try to be a pastor in order to get rich. And I can tell you that doesn't usually happen. This church has been so generous to my family through the years, to the point that most years there's an argument between me and the deacons and the elders because they wanted to raise my salary way higher than I think they should raise it — and I thank the Lord that that's the argument, not the other way around. But that's not the motivation. And I pray that the Lord will never let me remain here if the only thing I want from the church is a paycheck, because that would disqualify me as an elder in the church of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Third: not as lords over those entrusted but as examples to the flock. Elders are called to rule, and they receive their authority directly from Jesus Christ. But that does not mean they can misuse their authority and serve themselves as gods. In Ezekiel 34, there is a description of elders who decided that the sheep were there to serve them instead of them serving the sheep. And great destruction came upon Israel when that was the attitude. So elders are to exercise their authority — they are to be tough with the sheep at times — but for the sheep's good, not because they want the sheep to serve them.

Instead of having the flock serve the elders, they are to be examples to the flock. That's why there is such a high standard for elders. God is not saying elders need to be here, and everybody else is okay to be over there. He's saying, elders be here, because I want everybody else to be there. The qualifications for an elder are actually not the ceiling but the floor — what it means to be a mature Christian — and every man needs to aspire to that.

And then implicit in the command to shepherd the flock — if the elders are to shepherd, what is the other side of the coin? The sheep are to be shepherded. They are to follow the shepherds with joy and thanksgiving. They are not to resist the shepherding. This is made clear in verse 5 and throughout the New Testament, especially in Hebrews 13:17: obey those who rule over you and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls as those who must give account. Let them do so with joy and not with grief, for that would be unprofitable for you.

## The Reason for the Command

Now, why did Peter give us that command? Verse one starts with the word therefore — therefore I exhort the elders among you. Because of something Peter had said, these verses are exhorting the elders. And we find that reason in verses 17 through 19 of chapter four: because the judgment is coming upon the church of the Lord Jesus Christ. God doesn't start judgment in the world, because the world already stands condemned. The judgment starts in the church of the Lord Jesus Christ, the visible church.

And the church is not going to be much different from its leaders — and that's how God set up the church to be. As mentioned in Ezekiel 34: the shepherds were not doing their job, and the entire flock was judged for it. Do you get the awesome responsibility to stand before God and give an account for how we elders shepherd the flock? That's what we're going to have to do — stand before God and give account.

## The Blessing: The Crown of Glory (v. 4)

But as we're faithful as elders through the grace of God, blessings come from this command. There are blessings for a job well done. Christ himself, the chief shepherd of the sheep under whom we serve, will give the elders the crown of glory, as Peter says in verse 4. The elders will stand before Christ and Christ will say to them: well done, my good and faithful servant. You faithfully ministered to my flock; I now give you my glory.

And that's the desire of the elders of this church — to be recognized by Christ as faithful shepherds of his church. So elders and flock: follow the chief shepherd, obey him, love him, serve him — because there is nothing else in this dry and thirsty land that will bring you satisfaction. Today we learn that one of the ways we do that is by following the shepherds that he's placed over us when they are following Christ faithfully.

Let us pray together. Father, thank you for your word. We thank you that you are so detailed in your word, that you show how much you've given to us. We thank you that Christ is the head of the church, and has given us those that can shepherd us, can minister to us, can lead us. Father, whatever level we are, we pray that we'll be able to submit to those that have authority over us. We pray that you be with our elders. We pray to lead them, keep them faithful to Christ, keep them from attacks of Satan and keep them from disqualifying sins. Help us, Lord, to be servants like the Lord Jesus Christ. Help us to be able to lay our lives for the sheep. And we pray to bless the sheep. Father, pray that you bring them through us to still waters, safe waters to drink, so that they might have life. Help us to bring them to the living water who is the Lord Jesus Christ. And help us to lead them to pastures where they can feed peacefully and grow in Christ. We pray that you'd enable us to obey you by your grace, for asking in Jesus' name. Amen.
